message.content:
Hi. Last session we set up the whole white label site. Now it is complete (and looking great) plz review this reflective architecture setup & plan structure -   the idea is to have a good starting point with info to help organise new project - limited to the tech scope outlined in /docs/init/setup.txt - while being flexible and not restricting options / approaches more applicable to a specific project or bloating memory / context at the outset before info is helpful.


I’d like to text out the project gemini mcp Cowork/projects/white-hat-label/mcps/claude-add-models-mcp  - plz consult with Gemini specifically on this reflective architecture setup & plan structure -  focusing on the above points.

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

content.text: Let me first read the relevant files to understand the setup, then we'll test the Gemini MCP.

content.content: 1 # anthropicprinciple.ai — CLAUDE.md 2 3 > **Instruction file for Claude Code.** Single source of truth for how Claude should approach this project. Follow these instructions exactly and in preference to any defaults. 4 5 --- 6 7 ## Summary 8 9 10 --- 11 12 ## Stack 13 14 | Layer | Technology | 15 |-------|-----------| 16 | Markup | (semantic, W3C valid | 17 | Presentation | 18 | Behaviour | 19 | Fonts | 20 | Hosting | 21 | CI | 22 | Domain | 23 | Local dev | 24 25 26 --- 27 28 ## Repository & Deployment 29 30 | Item | Value | 31 |------|-------| 32 | GitHub / Gitlab | 33 | Live site | 34 | Branches | `dev` → `main` → production ( 35 | Git workflow | See [Git Workflow](#git-workflow) below | 36 37 --- 38 39 ## File Structure 40 41 ``` 42 ``` 43 44 ### ⚠️ Protected files — 45 46 --- 47 48 ## Technical Development Approach 49 50 - Separation of concerns 51 - Progressive enhancement 52 - Modular | OO 53 - Mobile-first 54 55 ### HTML 56 - **Separation of concerns.** HTML = structure and content. 57 58 ### CSS 59 - **CSS-first.** Use CSS for UI state wherever possible (`:checked`, `:focus-visible`, `@media`, custom properties, `@keyframes`). Add JS only when CSS cannot achieve the goal. 60 - **Separation of concerns.** CSS = all presentation. 61 - **CSS custom properties.** All colours, spacing, and timing values are defined as `--custom-properties` in `colors.css`. Never hard-code values in component CSS. 62 - **Mobile-first CSS.** Base styles target the smallest screen. Use `min-width` media queries to layer on complexity for larger screens. Never use `max-width` for responsive breakpoints. 63 - **Flex for layout, Grid for components.** Use Flexbox for page-level flow. CSS Grid for components where a two-dimensional structure is semantically correct (e.g. the clock grid, the 12-column page grid). 64 - **Chained classes.** Separate structural and presentational concerns using chained classes where it improves clarity. 65 66 ## JS 67 - **Modular JS.** Each JS file is a self-contained IIFE or module. No globals. No shared mutable state across files. 68 - **`localStorage` persistence.** 69 - **Separation of concerns.** JS = behaviour only. No inline styles. No inline event 70 --- 71 72 ## UX Goals 73 74 - **Immediate delight.** The animation plays instantly on load — no spinner, no splash screen. 75 - **Minimal chrome.** On the main page, the clock fills the full viewport. Nothing competes. 76 - **Discoverable controls.** `/clock-controls.html` is accessible via keyboard and linked clearly. 77 - **Settings persist automatically.** No save button needed — `localStorage` updates on every change. 78 - **Large touch targets.** All interactive controls ≥ 44 × 44 px. Labels, inputs, and buttons are large and readable. 79 - **Clean and minimal.** No decoration that doesn't serve the experience. 80 - **Orientation aware.** The clock grid adapts gracefully to landscape on small-screen devices. 81 - **Reduced motion respect.** The controls page respects `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce`. 82 - **Keyboard accessible.** Every interactive element reachable by Tab, operable by Enter/Space, dismissable by Escape. 83 84 --- 85 86 ## SEO Features 87 88 - Descriptive `` on every page. 89 - `<meta name="description">` on every page. 90 - Open Graph `og:` tags — title, description, type, image, url. 91 - `<link rel="canonical">` on every page. 92 - Schema.org JSON-LD structured data (`WebSite`, `WebApplication`). 93 - Semantic HTML5 landmarks: `<main>`, `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<footer>` where appropriate. 94 - `lang="en"` on `<html>`. 95 - `<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">` on indexable pages. 96 - `site.webmanifest` for PWA and search discoverability. 97 98 --- 99 100 ## Performance Features 101 102 - All `<img>` elements have `width`, `height`, `alt`; below-fold images use `loading="lazy"`. 103 - `srcset` with `250w` and `500w` resolution variants; `sizes` reflects layout breakpoints. 104 - Explicit image dimensions prevent Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS). 105 - `defer` attribute on all `<script>` tags. 106 - `requestAnimationFrame` for the clock render loop — no layout-triggering properties animated. 107 - `logger.js` buffers log entries in memory — no synchronous `sessionStorage` I/O on every call. 108 - **Target: Google PageSpeed ≥ 95 on both mobile and desktop.** 109 110 --- 111 112 ## Content Summary 113 114 | Page | Content | 115 |------|---------| 116 117 118 --- 119 120 ## Layout & Style Guide 121 122 ### Grid 123 - **12-column grid** for page-level layouts (`.container`, `.row`, `.col-*`). 124 - **Gutter:** `1rem` (16px at browser default). 125 - **Max content width:** `1440px`. 126 - **Minimum element width:** `288px` (prevents over-compression on small devices). 127 - **Clock page:** single full-viewport Flexbox container — no page grid. 128 129 ### Typography 130 131 | Role | Font stack | Weight | 132 |------|-----------|--------| 133 | Headings h1–h6 | `Verdana, Geneva, sans-serif` | bold | 134 | Body, paragraphs | `Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif` | normal | 135 | Monospace / code | `'Courier New', Courier, monospace` | normal | 136 | Aside attribution | `'Gill Sans', 'Gill Sans MT', Calibri, 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif` | normal | 137 138 The aside attribution uses Gill Sans as a deliberate brand accent — it is a distinct design element separate from the main type system. All are system fonts; no external font requests are made. 139 140 - Base: `1rem = 16px` (browser default — do not override `font-size` on `html` or `body`). 141 - All font sizes in `em`. All spacing distances in `rem`. 142 - Responsive line height: `1.5` base; `1.7` at ≥ 768px. 143 144 ### Colour Palette 145 146 | Token | Light Mode | Dark Mode | 147 |-------|-----------|-----------| 148 | `--color-bg` | `#f5f6f7` | `#0e0f12` | 149 | `--color-bg-alt` | `#ebeef0` | `#1a1c20` | 150 | `--color-text` | `#1d1f24` | `#a1b1ca` | 151 | `--color-text-muted` | `#555e64` | `#7a8896` | 152 | `--color-btn-primary` | `#a1b1ca` | `#1d1f24` | 153 | `--color-text-on-btn-primary` | `#1d1f24` | `#a1b1ca` | 154 | `--color-btn-secondary` | `#3e3b1b` | `#ebe6b7` | 155 | `--color-text-on-btn-secondary` | `#a1b1ca` | `#1d1f24` | 156 | `--color-link` | `#1d1f24` | `#a1b1ca` | 157 | `--color-focus` | `#1d1f24` | `#a1b1ca` | 158 159 All tokens defined in `styles/colors.css`. All dark-mode overrides via `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)` and `[data-theme="dark"]`. 160 161 ### Spacing Scale 162 163 | Token | Value | px equivalent | 164 |-------|-------|--------------| 165 | `--space-xs` | `0.25rem` | 4px | 166 | `--space-sm` | `0.5rem` | 8px | 167 | `--space-md` | `1rem` | 16px | 168 | `--space-lg` | `1.5rem` | 24px | 169 | `--space-xl` | `2rem` | 32px | 170 | `--space-2xl` | `3rem` | 48px | 171 | `--space-3xl` | `4rem` | 64px | 172 173 ### Breakpoints (mobile-first, min-width only) 174 175 | Name | Value | Context | 176 |------|-------|---------| 177 | `sm` | `480px` | Large phones | 178 | `md` | `768px` | Tablets | 179 | `lg` | `1024px` | Laptops | 180 | `xl` | `1280px` | Desktops | 181 | `2xl` | `1440px` | Wide screens | 182 183 --- 184 185 ## CSS Conventions 186 187 - **File per concern.** One responsibility per stylesheet. Never mix concerns. 188 - **Load order for non-clock pages:** `colors.css` → `fonts.css` → `global.css` → `components.css` → `[page].css` → `utilities.css`. 189 - **Clock page only:** `clock.css` (self-contained — contains its own reset, tokens, grid, and animation styles). 190 - **Component naming pattern:** 191 - `.component` — root: `.card` 192 - `.component-element` — child: `.card-title`, `.card-body` 193 - `.mod-variant` — modifier: `.mod-featured` 194 - `.is-state` — JS-applied state: `.is-open`, `.is-active` 195 - `.js-hook` — JS selector only, never styled: `.js-toggle` 196 - `.u-utility` — utility: `.u-sr-only`, `.u-flex-center` 197 - **No inline styles.** Never use the `style` attribute. 198 - **No `!important`.** Find a lower-specificity solution. 199 - **Alphabetical properties** within each rule block. 200 - **`:focus-visible` not `:focus`** — keyboard focus indicators only. 201 - **Hover, focus, active states — no visited state.** 202 - **Mobile-first always.** `@media (min-width: …)` only. Never `max-width`. 203 - **`box-sizing: border-box`** in every stylesheet reset. 204 - **CSS custom properties** for every colour, spacing value, and transition duration. 205 206 --- 207 208 ## Standards Compliance 209 210 - HTML5, W3C valid. 211 - WCAG 2.1 AA. 212 - `lang="en"` on `<html>`. 213 - `<meta charset="UTF-8">` first in `<head>`. 214 - `<!doctype html>` lowercase. 215 - Semantic landmarks: `<main>`, `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<footer>`. 216 - Every `<input>` and `<textarea>` paired with a `<label for="…">`. 217 - Every `<img>` has `alt`, `width`, `height`. 218 - Keyboard: Tab → interactive elements, Enter/Space → activate, Escape → dismiss overlays. 219 - Visible `:focus-visible` ring on all interactive elements. 220 - WCAG AA colour contrast: 4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for UI components. 221 - `prefers-color-scheme` for automatic dark mode. 222 - `prefers-reduced-motion` for animation opt-out. 223 - Schema.org JSON-LD structured data on each page. 224 - One `<h1>` per page; heading hierarchy sequential. 225 - `defer` on all `<script>` tags. 226 227 --- 228 229 ## Skills 230 231 | Skill | When to invoke | 232 |-------|---------------| 233 | `frontend-standards` | Any time HTML, CSS, or JS is written, reviewed, or refactored | 234 | `git-commit-messaging` | Every commit — produces Conventional Commits format messages | 235 236 --- 237 238 ## Test Programme 239 240 After each significant change, run this review prompt: 241 242 > "Taking into account the best practices docs in the project `docs/` folder, please run a code review on what we have. Look for better code organisation, clean code improvements, performance improvements, browser memory usage issues, and a security check. Update the md docs and memory file then add and commit to git. Suggest any additional best practice improvements and anything important I may have forgotten. Check changes didn't break completed task functionality." 243 244 ### Mat's Checklist 245 - [ ] 246 247 ### Claude's Checklist 248 - [ ] HTML validates — W3C validator (no errors, no warnings) 249 - [ ] All `<img>` have `alt`, `width`, `height` 250 - [ ] All `<input>` have an associated `<label for="…">` 251 - [ ] No `!important` in CSS 252 - [ ] No inline styles in HTML 253 - [ ] No `max-width` media queries (must be mobile-first `min-width`) 254 - [ ] `box-sizing: border-box` present in every stylesheet reset 255 - [ ] CSS custom properties used for all colours and spacing values 256 - [ ] `defer` on all `<script>` tags 257 - [ ] No console errors or warnings in browser devtools 258 - [ ] `prefers-color-scheme: dark` verified visually 259 - [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` verified (animations pause/reduce) 260 - [ ] Schema.org JSON-LD present on each page 261 - [ ] Open Graph meta tags present on each page 262 263 --- 264 265 ## Common Commands 266 267 ```bash 268 # Local dev server 269 npx live-server 270 271 # Validate HTML (requires html-validate) 272 npx html-validate index.html clock-controls.html 273 274 # Show staged + unstaged diff before committing 275 git diff 276 277 # Show what would be committed 278 git diff --cached 279 280 # Stage specific files and commit 281 git add styles/colors.css styles/global.css 282 git commit -m "style(css): mobile-first refactor and brand colour update" 283 ``` 284 285 --- 286 287 ## Git Workflow 288 289 290 - **Commit messages:** Conventional Commits — `type(scope): description` 291 - Types: `feat` `fix` `style` `refactor` `docs` `perf` `test` `chore` 292 - Examples: `feat(clock): add pattern 5` · `fix(controls): persist UTC offset` · `style(css): mobile-first refactor` 293 - **Always `git diff` before committing** — review every change. 294 - **Stop and show `git diff` after each task** — confirm with user before proceeding. 295 - **`git push` requires explicit user confirmation** — push deploys immediately to production. 296 - Use the `git-commit-messaging` skill for all commit messages. 297 298 --- 299 300 ## Logging System 301 302 `js/logger.js` provides a structured development logger, kept entirely separate from application code. 303 304 ```js 305 // Log a named event with optional data payload 306 Logger.log('section', 'message', { optional: 'data' }); 307 308 // Time measurements 309 Logger.time('hero-image'); // start a named timer 310 Logger.timeEnd('hero-image'); // stop timer, log elapsed ms 311 312 // Inspect stored logs 313 Logger.dump(); // print all session logs to console 314 Logger.clear(); // clear sessionStorage log store 315 ``` 316 317 - Entries buffered in memory — no synchronous storage I/O on `log()` calls. 318 - On `beforeunload`, buffer is flushed to `sessionStorage` and a summary snapshot written to `localStorage`. 319 - Logger is passive: it never throws, never blocks execution. 320 - `Logger.flush()` — write buffer to sessionStorage immediately (e.g. before an expected crash). 321 - Remove `<script src="js/logger.js">` from HTML before production deployment if not needed. 322 [result-id: r2]

file.content: # anthropicprinciple.ai — CLAUDE.md > **Instruction file for Claude Code.** Single source of truth for how Claude should approach this project. Follow these instructions exactly and in preference to any defaults. --- ## Summary --- ## Stack | Layer | Technology | |-------|-----------| | Markup | (semantic, W3C valid | | Presentation | | Behaviour | | Fonts | | Hosting | | CI | | Domain | | Local dev | --- ## Repository & Deployment | Item | Value | |------|-------| | GitHub / Gitlab | | Live site | | Branches | `dev` → `main` → production ( | Git workflow | See [Git Workflow](#git-workflow) below | --- ## File Structure ``` ``` ### ⚠️ Protected files — --- ## Technical Development Approach - Separation of concerns - Progressive enhancement - Modular | OO - Mobile-first ### HTML - **Separation of concerns.** HTML = structure and content. ### CSS - **CSS-first.** Use CSS for UI state wherever possible (`:checked`, `:focus-visible`, `@media`, custom properties, `@keyframes`). Add JS only when CSS cannot achieve the goal. - **Separation of concerns.** CSS = all presentation. - **CSS custom properties.** All colours, spacing, and timing values are defined as `--custom-properties` in `colors.css`. Never hard-code values in component CSS. - **Mobile-first CSS.** Base styles target the smallest screen. Use `min-width` media queries to layer on complexity for larger screens. Never use `max-width` for responsive breakpoints. - **Flex for layout, Grid for components.** Use Flexbox for page-level flow. CSS Grid for components where a two-dimensional structure is semantically correct (e.g. the clock grid, the 12-column page grid). - **Chained classes.** Separate structural and presentational concerns using chained classes where it improves clarity. ## JS - **Modular JS.** Each JS file is a self-contained IIFE or module. No globals. No shared mutable state across files. - **`localStorage` persistence.** - **Separation of concerns.** JS = behaviour only. No inline styles. No inline event --- ## UX Goals - **Immediate delight.** The animation plays instantly on load — no spinner, no splash screen. - **Minimal chrome.** On the main page, the clock fills the full viewport. Nothing competes. - **Discoverable controls.** `/clock-controls.html` is accessible via keyboard and linked clearly. - **Settings persist automatically.** No save button needed — `localStorage` updates on every change. - **Large touch targets.** All interactive controls ≥ 44 × 44 px. Labels, inputs, and buttons are large and readable. - **Clean and minimal.** No decoration that doesn't serve the experience. - **Orientation aware.** The clock grid adapts gracefully to landscape on small-screen devices. - **Reduced motion respect.** The controls page respects `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce`. - **Keyboard accessible.** Every interactive element reachable by Tab, operable by Enter/Space, dismissable by Escape. --- ## SEO Features - Descriptive `` on every page. - `<meta name="description">` on every page. - Open Graph `og:` tags — title, description, type, image, url. - `<link rel="canonical">` on every page. - Schema.org JSON-LD structured data (`WebSite`, `WebApplication`). - Semantic HTML5 landmarks: `<main>`, `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<footer>` where appropriate. - `lang="en"` on `<html>`. - `<meta name="robots" content="index, follow">` on indexable pages. - `site.webmanifest` for PWA and search discoverability. --- ## Performance Features - All `<img>` elements have `width`, `height`, `alt`; below-fold images use `loading="lazy"`. - `srcset` with `250w` and `500w` resolution variants; `sizes` reflects layout breakpoints. - Explicit image dimensions prevent Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS). - `defer` attribute on all `<script>` tags. - `requestAnimationFrame` for the clock render loop — no layout-triggering properties animated. - `logger.js` buffers log entries in memory — no synchronous `sessionStorage` I/O on every call. - **Target: Google PageSpeed ≥ 95 on both mobile and desktop.** --- ## Content Summary | Page | Content | |------|---------| --- ## Layout & Style Guide ### Grid - **12-column grid** for page-level layouts (`.container`, `.row`, `.col-*`). - **Gutter:** `1rem` (16px at browser default). - **Max content width:** `1440px`. - **Minimum element width:** `288px` (prevents over-compression on small devices). - **Clock page:** single full-viewport Flexbox container — no page grid. ### Typography | Role | Font stack | Weight | |------|-----------|--------| | Headings h1–h6 | `Verdana, Geneva, sans-serif` | bold | | Body, paragraphs | `Arial, Helvetica, sans-serif` | normal | | Monospace / code | `'Courier New', Courier, monospace` | normal | | Aside attribution | `'Gill Sans', 'Gill Sans MT', Calibri, 'Trebuchet MS', sans-serif` | normal | The aside attribution uses Gill Sans as a deliberate brand accent — it is a distinct design element separate from the main type system. All are system fonts; no external font requests are made. - Base: `1rem = 16px` (browser default — do not override `font-size` on `html` or `body`). - All font sizes in `em`. All spacing distances in `rem`. - Responsive line height: `1.5` base; `1.7` at ≥ 768px. ### Colour Palette | Token | Light Mode | Dark Mode | |-------|-----------|-----------| | `--color-bg` | `#f5f6f7` | `#0e0f12` | | `--color-bg-alt` | `#ebeef0` | `#1a1c20` | | `--color-text` | `#1d1f24` | `#a1b1ca` | | `--color-text-muted` | `#555e64` | `#7a8896` | | `--color-btn-primary` | `#a1b1ca` | `#1d1f24` | | `--color-text-on-btn-primary` | `#1d1f24` | `#a1b1ca` | | `--color-btn-secondary` | `#3e3b1b` | `#ebe6b7` | | `--color-text-on-btn-secondary` | `#a1b1ca` | `#1d1f24` | | `--color-link` | `#1d1f24` | `#a1b1ca` | | `--color-focus` | `#1d1f24` | `#a1b1ca` | All tokens defined in `styles/colors.css`. All dark-mode overrides via `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)` and `[data-theme="dark"]`. ### Spacing Scale | Token | Value | px equivalent | |-------|-------|--------------| | `--space-xs` | `0.25rem` | 4px | | `--space-sm` | `0.5rem` | 8px | | `--space-md` | `1rem` | 16px | | `--space-lg` | `1.5rem` | 24px | | `--space-xl` | `2rem` | 32px | | `--space-2xl` | `3rem` | 48px | | `--space-3xl` | `4rem` | 64px | ### Breakpoints (mobile-first, min-width only) | Name | Value | Context | |------|-------|---------| | `sm` | `480px` | Large phones | | `md` | `768px` | Tablets | | `lg` | `1024px` | Laptops | | `xl` | `1280px` | Desktops | | `2xl` | `1440px` | Wide screens | --- ## CSS Conventions - **File per concern.** One responsibility per stylesheet. Never mix concerns. - **Load order for non-clock pages:** `colors.css` → `fonts.css` → `global.css` → `components.css` → `[page].css` → `utilities.css`. - **Clock page only:** `clock.css` (self-contained — contains its own reset, tokens, grid, and animation styles). - **Component naming pattern:** - `.component` — root: `.card` - `.component-element` — child: `.card-title`, `.card-body` - `.mod-variant` — modifier: `.mod-featured` - `.is-state` — JS-applied state: `.is-open`, `.is-active` - `.js-hook` — JS selector only, never styled: `.js-toggle` - `.u-utility` — utility: `.u-sr-only`, `.u-flex-center` - **No inline styles.** Never use the `style` attribute. - **No `!important`.** Find a lower-specificity solution. - **Alphabetical properties** within each rule block. - **`:focus-visible` not `:focus`** — keyboard focus indicators only. - **Hover, focus, active states — no visited state.** - **Mobile-first always.** `@media (min-width: …)` only. Never `max-width`. - **`box-sizing: border-box`** in every stylesheet reset. - **CSS custom properties** for every colour, spacing value, and transition duration. --- ## Standards Compliance - HTML5, W3C valid. - WCAG 2.1 AA. - `lang="en"` on `<html>`. - `<meta charset="UTF-8">` first in `<head>`. - `<!doctype html>` lowercase. - Semantic landmarks: `<main>`, `<header>`, `<nav>`, `<footer>`. - Every `<input>` and `<textarea>` paired with a `<label for="…">`. - Every `<img>` has `alt`, `width`, `height`. - Keyboard: Tab → interactive elements, Enter/Space → activate, Escape → dismiss overlays. - Visible `:focus-visible` ring on all interactive elements. - WCAG AA colour contrast: 4.5:1 for text, 3:1 for UI components. - `prefers-color-scheme` for automatic dark mode. - `prefers-reduced-motion` for animation opt-out. - Schema.org JSON-LD structured data on each page. - One `<h1>` per page; heading hierarchy sequential. - `defer` on all `<script>` tags. --- ## Skills | Skill | When to invoke | |-------|---------------| | `frontend-standards` | Any time HTML, CSS, or JS is written, reviewed, or refactored | | `git-commit-messaging` | Every commit — produces Conventional Commits format messages | --- ## Test Programme After each significant change, run this review prompt: > "Taking into account the best practices docs in the project `docs/` folder, please run a code review on what we have. Look for better code organisation, clean code improvements, performance improvements, browser memory usage issues, and a security check. Update the md docs and memory file then add and commit to git. Suggest any additional best practice improvements and anything important I may have forgotten. Check changes didn't break completed task functionality." ### Mat's Checklist - [ ] ### Claude's Checklist - [ ] HTML validates — W3C validator (no errors, no warnings) - [ ] All `<img>` have `alt`, `width`, `height` - [ ] All `<input>` have an associated `<label for="…">` - [ ] No `!important` in CSS - [ ] No inline styles in HTML - [ ] No `max-width` media queries (must be mobile-first `min-width`) - [ ] `box-sizing: border-box` present in every stylesheet reset - [ ] CSS custom properties used for all colours and spacing values - [ ] `defer` on all `<script>` tags - [ ] No console errors or warnings in browser devtools - [ ] `prefers-color-scheme: dark` verified visually - [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion: reduce` verified (animations pause/reduce) - [ ] Schema.org JSON-LD present on each page - [ ] Open Graph meta tags present on each page --- ## Common Commands ```bash # Local dev server npx live-server # Validate HTML (requires html-validate) npx html-validate index.html clock-controls.html # Show staged + unstaged diff before committing git diff # Show what would be committed git diff --cached # Stage specific files and commit git add styles/colors.css styles/global.css git commit -m "style(css): mobile-first refactor and brand colour update" ``` --- ## Git Workflow - **Commit messages:** Conventional Commits — `type(scope): description` - Types: `feat` `fix` `style` `refactor` `docs` `perf` `test` `chore` - Examples: `feat(clock): add pattern 5` · `fix(controls): persist UTC offset` · `style(css): mobile-first refactor` - **Always `git diff` before committing** — review every change. - **Stop and show `git diff` after each task** — confirm with user before proceeding. - **`git push` requires explicit user confirmation** — push deploys immediately to production. - Use the `git-commit-messaging` skill for all commit messages. --- ## Logging System `js/logger.js` provides a structured development logger, kept entirely separate from application code. ```js // Log a named event with optional data payload Logger.log('section', 'message', { optional: 'data' }); // Time measurements Logger.time('hero-image'); // start a named timer Logger.timeEnd('hero-image'); // stop timer, log elapsed ms // Inspect stored logs Logger.dump(); // print all session logs to console Logger.clear(); // clear sessionStorage log store ``` - Entries buffered in memory — no synchronous storage I/O on `log()` calls. - On `beforeunload`, buffer is flushed to `sessionStorage` and a summary snapshot written to `localStorage`. - Logger is passive: it never throws, never blocks execution. - `Logger.flush()` — write buffer to sessionStorage immediately (e.g. before an expected crash). - Remove `<script src="js/logger.js">` from HTML before production deployment if not needed.

content.content:
1	# CLAUDE.md - GLM-5 MCP Server Project Memory
2	
3	## Project Overview
4	
5	**Purpose:** MCP server that integrates Z.ai's GLM-5 (744B parameter model) with Claude Desktop to reduce Claude API consumption by 10x through intelligent task delegation.
6	
7	**Core Value:** Solve the "weekly Claude Pro limit exhausted in 2 days" problem by using a hybrid orchestration approach where Claude (Sonnet 4.5 or Opus 4.6) coordinates while GLM-5 handles heavy computational tasks.
8	
9	**Key Metrics:**
10	- 10x reduction in Claude consumption
11	- 18-30x ROI improvement on Claude Pro subscriptions
12	- 100% availability (no more multi-day blockages)
13	
14	## Architecture
15	
16	### Hybrid Orchestration Model
17	
18	```
19	User Request
20	    ↓
21	Claude / Opus (Parent)
22	    ├─→ Planning & Coordination (stays in Claude)
23	    ├─→ File Operations (stays in Claude)
24	    ├─→ Quick Responses <100 words (stays in Claude)
25	    │
26	    ├─→ PRIORITY 1: Spawn parallel sub-agents (for multi-part tasks)
27	    │    └─→ Each sub-agent uses GLM-5 for code/analysis
28	    │         ├─→ ask_glm5_pro for code generation
29	    │         ├─→ ask_glm5 for analysis/docs
30	    │         └─→ Sub-agent writes output to disk
31	    │
32	    └─→ PRIORITY 2: Delegate to GLM-5 directly (for single-unit tasks)
33	         ├─→ Complex Analysis (>300 words)
34	         ├─→ Code Generation (>50 lines)
35	         ├─→ Research Synthesis
36	         └─→ Document Processing
37	```
38	
39	### Technology Stack
40	
41	- **MCP Server:** Node.js + @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
42	- **API Provider:** Z.ai (https://api.z.ai/api/paas/v4)
43	- **Models Used:**
44	  - GLM-5 (744B params) - General reasoning & coding
45	  - GLM-4.6V - Vision & OCR tasks
46	  - search-prime - Web search engine
47	
48	## GLM-5 Delegation Gate (MANDATORY)
49	
50	**STOP. Run this checklist BEFORE writing code or documentation. This applies to you AND to any Task sub-agents you spawn.**
51	
52	### Execution Priority (follow this order)
53	
54	**When facing a task with multiple independent parts:**
55	1. **FIRST: Spawn parallel sub-agents** — each sub-agent uses GLM-5 for its heavy work. This is the fastest path and MUST be preferred over sequential GLM-5 calls.
56	2. **SECOND: Delegate to GLM-5 directly** — when the task is a single unit that cannot be parallelized, use `ask_glm5` or `ask_glm5_pro`.
57	3. **LAST RESORT: Do it yourself** — only for orchestration, file I/O, client polish, and responses <100 words. Opus/Claude should almost never generate content directly.
58	
59	### Pre-Flight Checks
60	
61	1. **Am I (or my sub-agent) generating >50 lines of code?**
62	   → Use `ask_glm5_pro` to generate the code, then write the output to disk.
63	
64	2. **Am I (or my sub-agent) writing >300 words of analysis, docs, or summaries?**
65	   → Use `ask_glm5` to generate the content, then write the output to disk.
66	
67	3. **Task sub-agents MUST also use GLM-5.**
68	   Sub-agents have access to all MCP tools including `ask_glm5_pro` and `ask_glm5`. When spawning a Task sub-agent, **explicitly instruct it to use GLM-5 for code generation and analysis** in the prompt. Sub-agents that generate >50 lines of code or >300 words of content themselves (without delegating to GLM-5) are violating this rule.
69	
70	4. **Pattern: GLM-5 generates → Claude/sub-agent writes to disk.**
71	   Claude's role (parent or sub-agent) is orchestration + file I/O. GLM-5's role is content generation.
72	
73	**Violation of this gate wastes the user's Anthropic budget. There is no acceptable excuse.**
74	
75	---
76	
77	## Tool Delegation Guidelines
78	
79	### When to Use Each Tool
80	
81	#### `ask_glm5` - Complex Reasoning
82	**ALWAYS delegate when:**
83	- Analysis requires >300 words output
84	- Multi-step logical reasoning chains
85	- System design or architecture decisions
86	- Strategic analysis or synthesis
87	- Research report generation (>1000 words)
88	
89	**Examples:**
90	```javascript
91	// Good delegation
92	"Analyze the competitive landscape of AI agent platforms. Include market size,
93	key players, differentiation strategies, and growth projections."
94	
95	// Poor delegation (too simple, keep in Claude)
96	"What is an AI agent?"
97	```
98	
99	**Parameters:**
100	- `temperature`: 0.3-0.5 for analytical tasks, 0.7-0.9 for creative tasks
101	- `max_tokens`: 4000 default, increase to 8000 for comprehensive reports
102	
103	#### `ask_glm5_pro` - Code Generation
104	**ALWAYS delegate when:**
105	- Generating >50 lines of code
106	- Complex algorithms or data structures
107	- Full component implementations
108	- Refactoring large code sections
109	- Code optimization or performance improvements
110	
111	**Examples:**
112	```javascript
113	// Good delegation
114	"Implement a React component for OAuth authentication with Google, GitHub,
115	and email/password. Include form validation, error handling, loading states,
116	and token management."
117	
118	// Poor delegation (keep in Claude)
119	"Fix this typo in the button text"
120	```
121	
122	**System Prompt Customization:**
123	```javascript
124	ask_glm5_pro({
125	  prompt: "Build a REST API...",
126	  system_prompt: "You are a senior backend engineer specializing in Node.js
127	                  and PostgreSQL. Follow best practices for error handling,
128	                  input validation, and security."
129	})
130	```
131	
132	#### `web_search` - Intelligence Gathering
133	**ALWAYS delegate when:**
134	- Need >3 sources of information
135	- Competitive intelligence research
136	- Market trends or real-time data
137	- News and current events
138	- Finding multiple perspectives
139	
140	**Parameters:**
141	- `count`: 5-10 for quick checks, 30-50 for deep research
142	- `search_recency_filter`:
143	  - "oneDay" for breaking news
144	  - "oneWeek" for recent developments
145	  - "oneMonth" for current trends
146	  - "noLimit" for comprehensive research
147	
148	**Examples:**
149	```javascript
150	// Competitive intelligence
151	web_search({
152	  search_query: "OpenAI GPT-5 launch features pricing 2024",
153	  count: 30,
154	  search_recency_filter: "oneMonth"
155	})
156	
157	// Domain-filtered research
158	web_search({
159	  search_query: "MCP Model Context Protocol tutorials",
160	  search_domain_filter: "github.com,modelcontextprotocol.io"
161	})
162	```
163	
164	#### `web_reader` - Content Extraction
165	**ALWAYS use after web_search to:**
166	- Extract full article content
167	- Read competitor documentation
168	- Analyze landing pages
169	- Parse blog posts for detailed information
170	
171	**Workflow:**
172	```javascript
173	// 1. Search for sources
174	web_search("AI agent market analysis 2024", count=10)
175	
176	// 2. Read top 3-5 articles
177	web_reader(url1, return_format="markdown")
178	web_reader(url2, return_format="markdown")
179	web_reader(url3, return_format="markdown")
180	
181	// 3. Synthesize with GLM-5
182	ask_glm5("Based on these articles: [content], create an executive summary...")
183	```
184	
185	#### `parse_document` - OCR & Document Processing
186	**ALWAYS delegate when:**
187	- Extracting text from PDFs
188	- Processing scanned documents
189	- Reading invoices, receipts, business cards
190	- Analyzing multi-page contracts or proposals
191	
192	**Examples:**
193	```javascript
194	// Contract analysis
195	parse_document({
196	  file_url: "https://example.com/contract.pdf",
197	  return_format: "markdown"
198	})
199	// Then analyze with GLM-5
200	ask_glm5("Extract key obligations, risks, dates, and payment terms from this contract...")
201	
202	// Batch processing
203	parse_document(invoice1_url)
204	parse_document(invoice2_url)
205	parse_document(invoice3_url)
206	// Then summarize
207	ask_glm5("Create a summary table of these invoices with vendor, amount, date, status")
208	```
209	
210	## Model Selection Strategy
211	
212	### Use Sonnet 4.5 When:
213	✅ **Working with files and codebase**
214	- File operations (read, write, edit)
215	- Git operations
216	- Code navigation and search
217	- Quick code fixes (<50 lines, otherwise use ask_glm5_pro)
218	
219	✅ **Orchestration and planning**
220	- Breaking down user requests
221	- Coordinating multiple tool calls
222	- Presenting results to user
223	- Managing conversation flow
224	
225	✅ **Quick responses**
226	- Simple questions (<100 word answers)
227	- Clarifications
228	- Status updates
229	- Command execution
230	
231	### Use Opus 4.6 When:
232	✅ **Complex orchestration needed**
233	- Multi-step workflows with many decision points
234	- Advanced planning requiring deep reasoning
235	- Coordinating 5+ tool calls with dependencies
236	- Strategic architecture decisions
237	
238	✅ **Execution priority for Opus:**
239	- **Priority 1:** Spawn parallel sub-agents for independent tasks (each sub-agent uses GLM-5)
240	- **Priority 2:** Delegate to GLM-5 directly when task is single-unit
241	- **Priority 3 (last resort):** Opus does it itself — only for orchestration, file I/O, <100 words
242	
243	**Opus Optimization Strategy:**
244	```
245	Opus = Architect & Coordinator (NEVER generates content directly)
246	Sub-agents = Parallel workers (MUST use GLM-5 for heavy work)
247	GLM-5 = Content engine (code, analysis, docs)
248	
249	Parallelism FIRST → GLM-5 delegation SECOND → Opus self-execution LAST
250	```
251	
252	## Consumption Optimization Rules
253	
254	### Critical Rules for Both Sonnet and Opus
255	
256	1. **Never synthesize in Claude what GLM-5 can do**
257	   - ❌ Bad: Claude reads 10 articles and writes 2000-word analysis
258	   - ✅ Good: web_reader fetches articles → ask_glm5 synthesizes
259	
260	2. **Delegate all multi-document analysis**
261	   - ❌ Bad: Claude analyzes 3 PDFs and compares
262	   - ✅ Good: parse_document x3 → ask_glm5 comparison
263	
264	3. **Use GLM-5 for first drafts, Claude for integration**
265	   - ❌ Bad: Claude generates entire React component
266	   - ✅ Good: ask_glm5_pro generates code → Claude integrates into project
267	
268	4. **Parallel tool usage when possible**
269	   - ✅ Call web_reader on multiple URLs simultaneously
270	   - ✅ Parse multiple documents in parallel
271	   - Then aggregate results with ask_glm5
272	
273	### Expected Consumption Patterns
274	
275	**Before GLM-5 MCP (Opus 4.6 only):**
276	```
277	Competitive analysis task:
278	- Search: 500 tokens
279	- Read 5 articles: 10,000 tokens
280	- Analysis: 8,000 tokens
281	Total: 18,500 tokens per task
282	```
283	
284	**After GLM-5 MCP (Opus + delegation):**
285	```
286	Same task:
287	- Opus orchestration: 800 tokens
288	- GLM web_search: 200 tokens (Z.ai)
289	- GLM web_reader: 1,500 tokens (Z.ai)
290	- GLM ask_glm5: 6,000 tokens (Z.ai)
291	Opus total: 800 tokens (96% reduction!)
292	```
293	
294	## Development Workflow
295	
296	### Adding New Tools
297	
298	1. **Research the API**
299	   - Read Z.ai docs: https://docs.z.ai
300	   - Test with curl/Postman first
301	   - Understand response format
302	
303	2. **Design the tool**
304	   - Choose verb-based name: `action_target`
305	   - Define clear parameters with defaults
306	   - Plan LLM-friendly output format
307	
308	3. **Implement**
309	   ```javascript
310	   // In ListToolsRequestSchema handler
311	   {
312	     name: "tool_name",
313	     description: "When to use + what it does + use cases",
314	     inputSchema: {
315	       type: "object",
316	       properties: { /* params */ },
317	       required: ["required_param"]
318	     }
319	   }
320	
321	   // In CallToolRequestSchema handler
322	   if (name === "tool_name") {
323	     try {
324	       // API call with error handling
325	       // Format response for LLM
326	       return { content: [{ type: "text", text: result }] };
327	     } catch (error) {
328	       console.error(`[Tool] Error:`, error);
329	       return {
330	         content: [{ type: "text", text: `Error: ${error.message}` }],
331	         isError: true
332	       };
333	     }
334	   }
335	   ```
336	
337	4. **Test thoroughly**
338	   - Happy path (correct input)
339	   - Error cases (invalid params, API errors)
340	   - Edge cases (empty results, timeouts)
341	   - End-to-end in Claude Desktop
342	
343	### Code Quality Standards
344	
345	**Error Handling:**
346	```javascript
347	// Always log context with errors
348	console.error(`[Tool Name] API Error ${response.status}:`, errorText);
349	
350	// Never expose API keys in errors
351	throw new Error(`API returned ${response.status}: ${errorText}`);
352	// NOT: throw new Error(`Failed with key ${API_KEY}`);
353	
354	// Return helpful error messages
355	return {
356	  content: [{
357	    type: "text",
358	    text: `Error in tool_name: ${error.message}\n\nTry: [helpful suggestion]`
359	  }],
360	  isError: true
361	};
362	```
363	
364	**Logging:**
365	```javascript
366	// Use console.error for debugging (goes to stderr, not user)
367	console.error(`[Tool Debug] Request:`, { param1, param2 });
368	console.error(`[Tool Debug] Response:`, { resultCount, status });
369	
370	// Never log sensitive data
371	console.error(`[Tool Debug] API Key:`, API_KEY); // ❌ NEVER
372	```
373	
374	**Parameter Validation:**
375	```javascript
376	// Validate at schema level when possible
377	{
378	  count: {
379	    type: "number",
380	    minimum: 1,
381	    maximum: 50,
382	    default: 10
383	  }
384	}
385	
386	// Runtime validation for complex cases
387	if (!args.prompt || args.prompt.trim().length === 0) {
388	  throw new Error("Prompt cannot be empty");
389	}
390	```
391	
392	## API Reference
393	
394	### Z.ai Endpoints Used
395	
396	**Base URL:** `https://api.z.ai/api/paas/v4`
397	
398	**Authentication:**
399	```javascript
400	headers: {
401	  "Content-Type": "application/json",
402	  "Authorization": `Bearer ${process.env.ZAI_API_KEY}`
403	}
404	```
405	
406	**Models:**
407	- `glm-5` - Latest flagship (744B params)
408	- `glm-4.7`, `glm-4.7-flash` - Previous generation
409	- `glm-4.6v` - Vision model for OCR
410	- `search-prime` - Web search engine
411	
412	**Rate Limits:**
413	- Check Z.ai dashboard for current limits
414	- Implement exponential backoff if needed
415	- Log rate limit warnings
416	
417	## Testing Guidelines
418	
419	### Manual Testing Checklist
420	
421	Before committing:
422	- [ ] Test in Claude Desktop (end-to-end)
423	- [ ] Test error cases (invalid input, network errors)
424	- [ ] Verify no API keys logged
425	- [ ] Check token consumption (should be minimal for orchestration)
426	- [ ] Test with both Sonnet 4.5 and Opus 4.6
427	
428	### Performance Testing
429	
430	Monitor Claude consumption:
431	```javascript
432	// Before GLM-5
433	console.log("Starting task with Claude only");
434	// Do task entirely in Claude
435	console.log("Tokens used:", /* check Claude usage */);
436	
437	// After GLM-5
438	console.log("Starting task with GLM-5 delegation");
439	// Delegate to GLM-5
440	console.log("Claude tokens used:", /* should be ~90% less */);
441	```
442	
443	## Security
444	
445	### API Key Management
446	
447	**Never commit:**
448	- `.env` files
449	- API keys in code
450	- Test API keys
451	- User credentials
452	
453	**Always:**
454	- Use environment variables: `process.env.ZAI_API_KEY`
455	- Add to `.gitignore`: `*apikey*`, `*api-key*`, `.env*`
456	- Validate keys exist at startup
457	- Fail gracefully if missing
458	
459	### Data Privacy
460	
461	- Never log user prompts in production
462	- Don't cache sensitive document content
463	- Respect Z.ai data retention policies
464	- Clear temp files after processing
465	
466	## Roadmap
467	
468	### Phase 1 (Current)
469	- [x] GLM-5 text generation (ask_glm5, ask_glm5_pro)
470	- [x] Web search (search-prime engine)
471	- [x] Web reader (markdown extraction)
472	- [x] Document parsing (GLM-OCR)
473	
474	### Phase 2 (Next)
475	- [ ] Translation agent (40+ languages)
476	- [ ] Slide generation (GLM Slide Agent)
477	- [ ] Streaming support for real-time responses
478	- [ ] Response caching for repeated queries
479	
480	### Phase 3 (Future)
481	- [ ] Image generation (GLM-Image)
482	- [ ] Audio transcription (GLM-ASR)
483	- [ ] Video analysis (GLM-4.6V video mode)
484	- [ ] Preset prompt templates
485	- [ ] Usage analytics dashboard
486	
487	## Common Patterns
488	
489	### Pattern 1: Research Pipeline
490	```javascript
491	// 1. Find sources
492	web_search("topic", count=30)
493	
494	// 2. Extract content (parallel)
495	Promise.all([
496	  web_reader(url1),
497	  web_reader(url2),
498	  web_reader(url3)
499	])
500	
501	// 3. Synthesize with GLM-5
502	ask_glm5("Analyze these sources and create executive summary...")
503	
504	// 4. Claude presents results
505	```
506	
507	### Pattern 2: Document Analysis
508	```javascript
509	// 1. Parse documents
510	parse_document(pdf_url, return_format="markdown")
511	
512	// 2. Analyze with GLM-5
513	ask_glm5("Extract key terms, obligations, risks from this contract...")
514	
515	// 3. Claude formats as structured output
516	```
517	
518	### Pattern 3: Code Generation + Integration
519	```javascript
520	// 1. GLM-5 generates implementation
521	ask_glm5_pro("Build a React authentication component...")
522	
523	// 2. Claude writes to files
524	Write(path, glm5_code)
525	
526	// 3. Claude runs tests
527	Bash("npm test")
528	
529	// 4. Claude commits
530	Bash("git add . && git commit")
531	```
532	
533	## Troubleshooting
534	
535	### Tools Not Appearing
536	
537	1. Check config path: `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json`
538	2. Verify absolute path to `index.js`
539	3. Restart Claude Desktop (quit completely, not just close)
540	4. Check stderr logs: `node index.js` in terminal
541	
542	### Empty GLM-5 Responses
543	
544	1. Check API key is valid and has credits
545	2. Verify thinking mode: `thinking: { type: "disabled" }`
546	3. Check both `content` and `reasoning_content` fields
547	4. Increase `max_tokens` if response is cut off
548	
549	### High Claude Consumption
550	
551	**Audit your workflow:**
552	- Are you delegating analysis tasks to GLM-5?
553	- Are you using web_reader before Claude processes content?
554	- Are you letting Claude generate >50 line code blocks?
555	- Are you using GLM-5 for multi-document synthesis?
556	- Are Task sub-agents using GLM-5 for their heavy work (not generating code/content themselves)?
557	
558	**Optimization checklist:**
559	- [ ] Analysis >300 words delegated to ask_glm5
560	- [ ] Code >50 lines uses ask_glm5_pro
561	- [ ] Research uses web_search → web_reader → ask_glm5
562	- [ ] Documents parsed with parse_document before analysis
563	- [ ] Task sub-agents use GLM-5 for heavy work (not generating code/content themselves)
564	- [ ] Claude only does orchestration + file ops
565	
566	## Contributing
567	
568	See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for:
569	- Code style guidelines
570	- PR process
571	- Testing requirements
572	- Tool design principles
573	
574	## Resources
575	
576	- [Z.ai API Docs](https://docs.z.ai)
577	- [MCP Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io)
578	- [Claude Desktop](https://claude.ai/download)
579	- [Repository](https://github.com/Arkya-AI/glm5-mcp)
580	
581	---
582	
583	**Remember:** The goal is to reduce Claude consumption while maintaining quality. Delegate heavy work, keep Claude for coordination. Both Sonnet 4.5 and Opus 4.6 benefit from this approach. **Task sub-agents are encouraged for parallelism — but each must use GLM-5 for code generation and analysis, not do it themselves.**
584

# CLAUDE.md - GLM-5 MCP Server Project Memory

## Project Overview

**Purpose:** MCP server that integrates Z.ai's GLM-5 (744B parameter model) with Claude Desktop to reduce Claude API consumption by 10x through intelligent task delegation.

**Core Value:** Solve the "weekly Claude Pro limit exhausted in 2 days" problem by using a hybrid orchestration approach where Claude (Sonnet 4.5 or Opus 4.6) coordinates while GLM-5 handles heavy computational tasks.

**Key Metrics:**
- 10x reduction in Claude consumption
- 18-30x ROI improvement on Claude Pro subscriptions
- 100% availability (no more multi-day blockages)

## Architecture

### Hybrid Orchestration Model

```
User Request
    ↓
Claude / Opus (Parent)
    ├─→ Planning & Coordination (stays in Claude)
    ├─→ File Operations (stays in Claude)
    ├─→ Quick Responses <100 words (stays in Claude)
    │
    ├─→ PRIORITY 1: Spawn parallel sub-agents (for multi-part tasks)
    │    └─→ Each sub-agent uses GLM-5 for code/analysis
    │         ├─→ ask_glm5_pro for code generation
    │         ├─→ ask_glm5 for analysis/docs
    │         └─→ Sub-agent writes output to disk
    │
    └─→ PRIORITY 2: Delegate to GLM-5 directly (for single-unit tasks)
         ├─→ Complex Analysis (>300 words)
         ├─→ Code Generation (>50 lines)
         ├─→ Research Synthesis
         └─→ Document Processing
```

### Technology Stack

- **MCP Server:** Node.js + @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
- **API Provider:** Z.ai (https://api.z.ai/api/paas/v4)
- **Models Used:**
  - GLM-5 (744B params) - General reasoning & coding
  - GLM-4.6V - Vision & OCR tasks
  - search-prime - Web search engine

## GLM-5 Delegation Gate (MANDATORY)

**STOP. Run this checklist BEFORE writing code or documentation. This applies to you AND to any Task sub-agents you spawn.**

### Execution Priority (follow this order)

**When facing a task with multiple independent parts:**
1. **FIRST: Spawn parallel sub-agents** — each sub-agent uses GLM-5 for its heavy work. This is the fastest path and MUST be preferred over sequential GLM-5 calls.
2. **SECOND: Delegate to GLM-5 directly** — when the task is a single unit that cannot be parallelized, use `ask_glm5` or `ask_glm5_pro`.
3. **LAST RESORT: Do it yourself** — only for orchestration, file I/O, client polish, and responses <100 words. Opus/Claude should almost never generate content directly.

### Pre-Flight Checks

1. **Am I (or my sub-agent) generating >50 lines of code?**
   → Use `ask_glm5_pro` to generate the code, then write the output to disk.

2. **Am I (or my sub-agent) writing >300 words of analysis, docs, or summaries?**
   → Use `ask_glm5` to generate the content, then write the output to disk.

3. **Task sub-agents MUST also use GLM-5.**
   Sub-agents have access to all MCP tools including `ask_glm5_pro` and `ask_glm5`. When spawning a Task sub-agent, **explicitly instruct it to use GLM-5 for code generation and analysis** in the prompt. Sub-agents that generate >50 lines of code or >300 words of content themselves (without delegating to GLM-5) are violating this rule.

4. **Pattern: GLM-5 generates → Claude/sub-agent writes to disk.**
   Claude's role (parent or sub-agent) is orchestration + file I/O. GLM-5's role is content generation.

**Violation of this gate wastes the user's Anthropic budget. There is no acceptable excuse.**

---

## Tool Delegation Guidelines

### When to Use Each Tool

#### `ask_glm5` - Complex Reasoning
**ALWAYS delegate when:**
- Analysis requires >300 words output
- Multi-step logical reasoning chains
- System design or architecture decisions
- Strategic analysis or synthesis
- Research report generation (>1000 words)

**Examples:**
```javascript
// Good delegation
"Analyze the competitive landscape of AI agent platforms. Include market size,
key players, differentiation strategies, and growth projections."

// Poor delegation (too simple, keep in Claude)
"What is an AI agent?"
```

**Parameters:**
- `temperature`: 0.3-0.5 for analytical tasks, 0.7-0.9 for creative tasks
- `max_tokens`: 4000 default, increase to 8000 for comprehensive reports

#### `ask_glm5_pro` - Code Generation
**ALWAYS delegate when:**
- Generating >50 lines of code
- Complex algorithms or data structures
- Full component implementations
- Refactoring large code sections
- Code optimization or performance improvements

**Examples:**
```javascript
// Good delegation
"Implement a React component for OAuth authentication with Google, GitHub,
and email/password. Include form validation, error handling, loading states,
and token management."

// Poor delegation (keep in Claude)
"Fix this typo in the button text"
```

**System Prompt Customization:**
```javascript
ask_glm5_pro({
  prompt: "Build a REST API...",
  system_prompt: "You are a senior backend engineer specializing in Node.js
                  and PostgreSQL. Follow best practices for error handling,
                  input validation, and security."
})
```

#### `web_search` - Intelligence Gathering
**ALWAYS delegate when:**
- Need >3 sources of information
- Competitive intelligence research
- Market trends or real-time data
- News and current events
- Finding multiple perspectives

**Parameters:**
- `count`: 5-10 for quick checks, 30-50 for deep research
- `search_recency_filter`:
  - "oneDay" for breaking news
  - "oneWeek" for recent developments
  - "oneMonth" for current trends
  - "noLimit" for comprehensive research

**Examples:**
```javascript
// Competitive intelligence
web_search({
  search_query: "OpenAI GPT-5 launch features pricing 2024",
  count: 30,
  search_recency_filter: "oneMonth"
})

// Domain-filtered research
web_search({
  search_query: "MCP Model Context Protocol tutorials",
  search_domain_filter: "github.com,modelcontextprotocol.io"
})
```

#### `web_reader` - Content Extraction
**ALWAYS use after web_search to:**
- Extract full article content
- Read competitor documentation
- Analyze landing pages
- Parse blog posts for detailed information

**Workflow:**
```javascript
// 1. Search for sources
web_search("AI agent market analysis 2024", count=10)

// 2. Read top 3-5 articles
web_reader(url1, return_format="markdown")
web_reader(url2, return_format="markdown")
web_reader(url3, return_format="markdown")

// 3. Synthesize with GLM-5
ask_glm5("Based on these articles: [content], create an executive summary...")
```

#### `parse_document` - OCR & Document Processing
**ALWAYS delegate when:**
- Extracting text from PDFs
- Processing scanned documents
- Reading invoices, receipts, business cards
- Analyzing multi-page contracts or proposals

**Examples:**
```javascript
// Contract analysis
parse_document({
  file_url: "https://example.com/contract.pdf",
  return_format: "markdown"
})
// Then analyze with GLM-5
ask_glm5("Extract key obligations, risks, dates, and payment terms from this contract...")

// Batch processing
parse_document(invoice1_url)
parse_document(invoice2_url)
parse_document(invoice3_url)
// Then summarize
ask_glm5("Create a summary table of these invoices with vendor, amount, date, status")
```

## Model Selection Strategy

### Use Sonnet 4.5 When:
✅ **Working with files and codebase**
- File operations (read, write, edit)
- Git operations
- Code navigation and search
- Quick code fixes (<50 lines, otherwise use ask_glm5_pro)

✅ **Orchestration and planning**
- Breaking down user requests
- Coordinating multiple tool calls
- Presenting results to user
- Managing conversation flow

✅ **Quick responses**
- Simple questions (<100 word answers)
- Clarifications
- Status updates
- Command execution

### Use Opus 4.6 When:
✅ **Complex orchestration needed**
- Multi-step workflows with many decision points
- Advanced planning requiring deep reasoning
- Coordinating 5+ tool calls with dependencies
- Strategic architecture decisions

✅ **Execution priority for Opus:**
- **Priority 1:** Spawn parallel sub-agents for independent tasks (each sub-agent uses GLM-5)
- **Priority 2:** Delegate to GLM-5 directly when task is single-unit
- **Priority 3 (last resort):** Opus does it itself — only for orchestration, file I/O, <100 words

**Opus Optimization Strategy:**
```
Opus = Architect & Coordinator (NEVER generates content directly)
Sub-agents = Parallel workers (MUST use GLM-5 for heavy work)
GLM-5 = Content engine (code, analysis, docs)

Parallelism FIRST → GLM-5 delegation SECOND → Opus self-execution LAST
```

## Consumption Optimization Rules

### Critical Rules for Both Sonnet and Opus

1. **Never synthesize in Claude what GLM-5 can do**
   - ❌ Bad: Claude reads 10 articles and writes 2000-word analysis
   - ✅ Good: web_reader fetches articles → ask_glm5 synthesizes

2. **Delegate all multi-document analysis**
   - ❌ Bad: Claude analyzes 3 PDFs and compares
   - ✅ Good: parse_document x3 → ask_glm5 comparison

3. **Use GLM-5 for first drafts, Claude for integration**
   - ❌ Bad: Claude generates entire React component
   - ✅ Good: ask_glm5_pro generates code → Claude integrates into project

4. **Parallel tool usage when possible**
   - ✅ Call web_reader on multiple URLs simultaneously
   - ✅ Parse multiple documents in parallel
   - Then aggregate results with ask_glm5

### Expected Consumption Patterns

**Before GLM-5 MCP (Opus 4.6 only):**
```
Competitive analysis task:
- Search: 500 tokens
- Read 5 articles: 10,000 tokens
- Analysis: 8,000 tokens
Total: 18,500 tokens per task
```

**After GLM-5 MCP (Opus + delegation):**
```
Same task:
- Opus orchestration: 800 tokens
- GLM web_search: 200 tokens (Z.ai)
- GLM web_reader: 1,500 tokens (Z.ai)
- GLM ask_glm5: 6,000 tokens (Z.ai)
Opus total: 800 tokens (96% reduction!)
```

## Development Workflow

### Adding New Tools

1. **Research the API**
   - Read Z.ai docs: https://docs.z.ai
   - Test with curl/Postman first
   - Understand response format

2. **Design the tool**
   - Choose verb-based name: `action_target`
   - Define clear parameters with defaults
   - Plan LLM-friendly output format

3. **Implement**
   ```javascript
   // In ListToolsRequestSchema handler
   {
     name: "tool_name",
     description: "When to use + what it does + use cases",
     inputSchema: {
       type: "object",
       properties: { /* params */ },
       required: ["required_param"]
     }
   }

   // In CallToolRequestSchema handler
   if (name === "tool_name") {
     try {
       // API call with error handling
       // Format response for LLM
       return { content: [{ type: "text", text: result }] };
     } catch (error) {
       console.error(`[Tool] Error:`, error);
       return {
         content: [{ type: "text", text: `Error: ${error.message}` }],
         isError: true
       };
     }
   }
   ```

4. **Test thoroughly**
   - Happy path (correct input)
   - Error cases (invalid params, API errors)
   - Edge cases (empty results, timeouts)
   - End-to-end in Claude Desktop

### Code Quality Standards

**Error Handling:**
```javascript
// Always log context with errors
console.error(`[Tool Name] API Error ${response.status}:`, errorText);

// Never expose API keys in errors
throw new Error(`API returned ${response.status}: ${errorText}`);
// NOT: throw new Error(`Failed with key ${API_KEY}`);

// Return helpful error messages
return {
  content: [{
    type: "text",
    text: `Error in tool_name: ${error.message}\n\nTry: [helpful suggestion]`
  }],
  isError: true
};
```

**Logging:**
```javascript
// Use console.error for debugging (goes to stderr, not user)
console.error(`[Tool Debug] Request:`, { param1, param2 });
console.error(`[Tool Debug] Response:`, { resultCount, status });

// Never log sensitive data
console.error(`[Tool Debug] API Key:`, API_KEY); // ❌ NEVER
```

**Parameter Validation:**
```javascript
// Validate at schema level when possible
{
  count: {
    type: "number",
    minimum: 1,
    maximum: 50,
    default: 10
  }
}

// Runtime validation for complex cases
if (!args.prompt || args.prompt.trim().length === 0) {
  throw new Error("Prompt cannot be empty");
}
```

## API Reference

### Z.ai Endpoints Used

**Base URL:** `https://api.z.ai/api/paas/v4`

**Authentication:**
```javascript
headers: {
  "Content-Type": "application/json",
  "Authorization": `Bearer ${process.env.ZAI_API_KEY}`
}
```

**Models:**
- `glm-5` - Latest flagship (744B params)
- `glm-4.7`, `glm-4.7-flash` - Previous generation
- `glm-4.6v` - Vision model for OCR
- `search-prime` - Web search engine

**Rate Limits:**
- Check Z.ai dashboard for current limits
- Implement exponential backoff if needed
- Log rate limit warnings

## Testing Guidelines

### Manual Testing Checklist

Before committing:
- [ ] Test in Claude Desktop (end-to-end)
- [ ] Test error cases (invalid input, network errors)
- [ ] Verify no API keys logged
- [ ] Check token consumption (should be minimal for orchestration)
- [ ] Test with both Sonnet 4.5 and Opus 4.6

### Performance Testing

Monitor Claude consumption:
```javascript
// Before GLM-5
console.log("Starting task with Claude only");
// Do task entirely in Claude
console.log("Tokens used:", /* check Claude usage */);

// After GLM-5
console.log("Starting task with GLM-5 delegation");
// Delegate to GLM-5
console.log("Claude tokens used:", /* should be ~90% less */);
```

## Security

### API Key Management

**Never commit:**
- `.env` files
- API keys in code
- Test API keys
- User credentials

**Always:**
- Use environment variables: `process.env.ZAI_API_KEY`
- Add to `.gitignore`: `*apikey*`, `*api-key*`, `.env*`
- Validate keys exist at startup
- Fail gracefully if missing

### Data Privacy

- Never log user prompts in production
- Don't cache sensitive document content
- Respect Z.ai data retention policies
- Clear temp files after processing

## Roadmap

### Phase 1 (Current)
- [x] GLM-5 text generation (ask_glm5, ask_glm5_pro)
- [x] Web search (search-prime engine)
- [x] Web reader (markdown extraction)
- [x] Document parsing (GLM-OCR)

### Phase 2 (Next)
- [ ] Translation agent (40+ languages)
- [ ] Slide generation (GLM Slide Agent)
- [ ] Streaming support for real-time responses
- [ ] Response caching for repeated queries

### Phase 3 (Future)
- [ ] Image generation (GLM-Image)
- [ ] Audio transcription (GLM-ASR)
- [ ] Video analysis (GLM-4.6V video mode)
- [ ] Preset prompt templates
- [ ] Usage analytics dashboard

## Common Patterns

### Pattern 1: Research Pipeline
```javascript
// 1. Find sources
web_search("topic", count=30)

// 2. Extract content (parallel)
Promise.all([
  web_reader(url1),
  web_reader(url2),
  web_reader(url3)
])

// 3. Synthesize with GLM-5
ask_glm5("Analyze these sources and create executive summary...")

// 4. Claude presents results
```

### Pattern 2: Document Analysis
```javascript
// 1. Parse documents
parse_document(pdf_url, return_format="markdown")

// 2. Analyze with GLM-5
ask_glm5("Extract key terms, obligations, risks from this contract...")

// 3. Claude formats as structured output
```

### Pattern 3: Code Generation + Integration
```javascript
// 1. GLM-5 generates implementation
ask_glm5_pro("Build a React authentication component...")

// 2. Claude writes to files
Write(path, glm5_code)

// 3. Claude runs tests
Bash("npm test")

// 4. Claude commits
Bash("git add . && git commit")
```

## Troubleshooting

### Tools Not Appearing

1. Check config path: `~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json`
2. Verify absolute path to `index.js`
3. Restart Claude Desktop (quit completely, not just close)
4. Check stderr logs: `node index.js` in terminal

### Empty GLM-5 Responses

1. Check API key is valid and has credits
2. Verify thinking mode: `thinking: { type: "disabled" }`
3. Check both `content` and `reasoning_content` fields
4. Increase `max_tokens` if response is cut off

### High Claude Consumption

**Audit your workflow:**
- Are you delegating analysis tasks to GLM-5?
- Are you using web_reader before Claude processes content?
- Are you letting Claude generate >50 line code blocks?
- Are you using GLM-5 for multi-document synthesis?
- Are Task sub-agents using GLM-5 for their heavy work (not generating code/content themselves)?

**Optimization checklist:**
- [ ] Analysis >300 words delegated to ask_glm5
- [ ] Code >50 lines uses ask_glm5_pro
- [ ] Research uses web_search → web_reader → ask_glm5
- [ ] Documents parsed with parse_document before analysis
- [ ] Task sub-agents use GLM-5 for heavy work (not generating code/content themselves)
- [ ] Claude only does orchestration + file ops

## Contributing

See [CONTRIBUTING.md](CONTRIBUTING.md) for:
- Code style guidelines
- PR process
- Testing requirements
- Tool design principles

## Resources

- [Z.ai API Docs](https://docs.z.ai)
- [MCP Specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io)
- [Claude Desktop](https://claude.ai/download)
- [Repository](https://github.com/Arkya-AI/glm5-mcp)

---

**Remember:** The goal is to reduce Claude consumption while maintaining quality. Delegate heavy work, keep Claude for coordination. Both Sonnet 4.5 and Opus 4.6 benefit from this approach. **Task sub-agents are encouraged for parallelism — but each must use GLM-5 for code generation and analysis, not do it themselves.**

1	# CLAUDE.md - Gemini MCP Integration Project Memory
2	
3	## Project Overview
4	This project implements a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that integrates Google Gemini 3.x capabilities directly into the Claude Desktop environment. The primary objective is to create a high-efficiency hybrid intelligence system where Claude acts as the primary orchestrator and Gemini serves as the high-volume execution engine.
5	
6	### Core Value Proposition
7	- **10x Reduction in Claude Consumption:** By offloading heavy lifting (long-form writing, large-scale analysis, code generation) to Gemini, Claude's token usage is minimized.
8	- **90%+ Opus Savings:** High-cost Claude Opus cycles are reserved strictly for complex orchestration and final synthesis, while Gemini handles the bulk of the data processing.
9	- **Continuous Availability:** Leverages Gemini's high rate limits and cost-effective tiers to ensure the agentic workflow remains responsive even during high-load periods.
10	
11	### What This Is Not
12	- **Not a Standalone Chatbot:** This server is designed to be used *by* Claude, not as a replacement for the Claude interface.
13	- **Not a Bypass for Safety Filters:** All Gemini safety settings are active; this tool does not circumvent provider-level content restrictions.
14	- **Not a Persistent Database:** While it processes large amounts of data, it does not provide long-term vector storage or RAG memory outside of the current session context.
15	- **Not a Direct File System Access Tool:** Gemini tools return data to Claude; Claude remains the only entity authorized to write to the local filesystem.
16	
17	### Quick Start
18	1. **Prerequisites:** Node.js v18+, Google AI Studio API Key.
19	2. **Installation:** Clone the repository and run `npm install`.
20	3. **Build:** Run `npm run build` to generate the distribution files.
21	4. **Configuration:** Add the server to your `claude_desktop_config.json` (see Configuration section).
22	5. **Verification:** Open Claude Desktop and look for the Gemini MCP tools. Test with: "Use Gemini to summarize the latest news on MCP."
23	
24	### Key Metrics
25	- **Target Token Reduction:** 10:1 ratio (Gemini tokens to Claude tokens).
26	- **Efficiency Goal:** 90% reduction in Claude-native processing for tasks exceeding 500 words or 100 lines of code.
27	- **Reliability:** 99% tool call success rate through robust error handling and fallback logic.
28	
29	---
30	
31	## Architecture
32	The integration utilizes a dual-path communication strategy to maximize feature availability while maintaining compatibility.
33	
34	### Data Flow
35	1. **User Input:** User provides a prompt to Claude Desktop.
36	2. **Orchestration:** Claude determines if the task requires high-volume processing or external data.
37	3. **Tool Call:** Claude sends a JSON-RPC request to the MCP Server.
38	4. **API Routing:** The MCP Server routes the request to either the OpenAI SDK path or the Native Gemini API path.
39	5. **Execution:** Gemini processes the request (optionally using Google Search grounding).
40	6. **Return Path:** Gemini Response -> MCP Server -> Claude Desktop.
41	7. **Final Synthesis:** Claude reviews the Gemini output and presents it to the user or performs file operations.
42	
43	### Hybrid Communication Approach
44	1. **OpenAI SDK Path:** Used for standard chat-based tools (`ask_gemini`, `ask_gemini_pro`). This provides a stable, standardized interface for text-to-text operations and ensures compatibility with standard streaming protocols.
45	2. **Native Gemini API Path:** Used for advanced features including `google_search` grounding, `url_context`, and multi-modal document processing. This path bypasses the OpenAI compatibility layer to access Gemini-specific parameters like `dynamic_retrieval_config`.
46	
47	### OpenAI SDK vs. Native API Tradeoffs
48	- **OpenAI SDK:**
49	    - *Pros:* Simpler error handling, standardized response format, easier to swap models.
50	    - *Cons:* No access to Google Search grounding, limited multi-modal support.
51	- **Native API:**
52	    - *Pros:* Full access to Gemini 3.x features, grounding, system instructions, and safety settings.
53	    - *Cons:* More complex request/response structure, requires specific SDK management.
54	
55	### Model Specifications
56	- **gemini-3-flash-preview:**
57	    - **Role:** General purpose, high-speed execution.
58	    - **Default Output Tokens:** 8,192.
59	    - **Use Case:** Summarization, basic research, drafting, and sub-agent tasks.
60	- **gemini-3-pro-preview:**
61	    - **Role:** Complex reasoning and heavy code generation.
62	    - **Default Output Tokens:** 16,384.
63	    - **Use Case:** Architectural design, complex debugging, deep technical analysis.
64	
65	### Core Toolset
66	1. `ask_gemini`: General text generation and reasoning via Flash.
67	2. `ask_gemini_pro`: High-complexity reasoning and code generation via Pro.
68	3. `web_search`: Real-time information retrieval using Google Search grounding.
69	4. `web_reader`: High-fidelity content extraction from specific URLs.
70	5. `parse_document`: Advanced OCR and structured data extraction from PDFs and images.
71	
72	### Configuration
73	To enable the server, add the following to your `claude_desktop_config.json`:
74	```json
75	{
76	  "mcpServers": {
77	    "gemini": {
78	      "command": "node",
79	      "args": ["/path/to/gemini-mcp/dist/index.js"],
80	      "env": {
81	        "GOOGLE_API_KEY": "your-api-key-here"
82	      }
83	    }
84	  }
85	}
86	```
87	Environment variables:
88	- `GOOGLE_API_KEY` (required): Your Google AI Studio API key. Generate one at https://aistudio.google.com/apikey.
89	- `GEMINI_MODEL_FLASH` (optional): Override the Flash model identifier. Defaults to `gemini-3-flash-preview`.
90	- `GEMINI_MODEL_PRO` (optional): Override the Pro model identifier. Defaults to `gemini-3-pro-preview`.
91	
92	---
93	
94	## Gemini Delegation Gate (MANDATORY)
95	Claude must evaluate every task against this gate before performing the work itself. This applies to Claude (parent) AND to any sub-agents spawned during task execution.
96	
97	### STOP Checklist
98	1. **Code Volume:** Will the output exceed 50 lines of code? -> **MUST** use `ask_gemini_pro`.
99	2. **Analysis Volume:** Will the analysis exceed 300 words? -> **MUST** use `ask_gemini`.
100	3. **Sub-Agent Requirement:** If the task requires multiple steps, sub-agents **MUST** be instructed to use Gemini tools for their internal processing.
101	4. **Pattern:** Gemini generates the content -> Claude reviews and writes to the local filesystem.
102	
103	### Execution Priority
104	1. **FIRST PRIORITY:** Spawn parallel sub-agents. Each sub-agent is assigned a specific segment of the task and must use Gemini tools for execution. This is the fastest path and must always be preferred when work can be parallelized.
105	2. **SECOND PRIORITY:** Direct delegation. If the task is a single unit of work (e.g., "Write this function"), call Gemini directly.
106	3. **LAST RESORT:** Claude performs the task itself. This is reserved ONLY for orchestration, final assembly, file I/O, or tasks under 100 words where Gemini overhead is not justified.
107	
108	### Violation Examples
109	- **Violation:** Claude writes a 200-line React component directly in the chat.
110	    - *Why it fails:* This consumes approximately 1,000 Claude output tokens that should be Gemini tokens.
111	    - *Correction:* Claude should call `ask_gemini_pro` with the component requirements, receive the code, and write it to disk.
112	- **Violation:** Claude summarizes a 50-page PDF using its own context window.
113	    - *Why it fails:* Claude reads and processes 50,000+ input tokens internally, then generates a 2,000-word summary.
114	    - *Correction:* Claude should use `parse_document` to extract text, then pass the extracted text to `ask_gemini` for summarization.
115	- **Violation:** A sub-agent generates a 500-word analysis without using Gemini.
116	    - *Why it fails:* Sub-agents have access to the same MCP tools as the parent. They must use them.
117	    - *Correction:* When spawning the sub-agent, include explicit instructions: "Use `ask_gemini` for any analysis over 300 words."
118	- **Violation:** Claude performs 5 consecutive web lookups using its native tools and then synthesizes the findings.
119	    - *Why it fails:* The research + synthesis can be handled entirely by `web_search` + `web_reader` + `ask_gemini`.
120	    - *Correction:* Use `web_search` for discovery, `web_reader` for extraction, `ask_gemini` for synthesis.
121	
122	### Self-Audit After Each Task
123	After completing any significant task, Claude must verify:
124	- [ ] Did I use more than 1,000 of my own output tokens for content generation? (If yes, delegation failed.)
125	- [ ] Did I perform a task that `ask_gemini` or `ask_gemini_pro` could have handled?
126	- [ ] Did I read web content natively instead of using `web_reader`?
127	- [ ] Did I process a PDF natively instead of using `parse_document`?
128	- [ ] Did my sub-agents use Gemini for their heavy work?
129	- [ ] Is the final output written to disk (not just in conversation)?
130

132	
133	## Tool Delegation Guidelines
134	
135	### ask_gemini (Flash)
136	
137	**Role:** High-speed, low-cost execution for standard analytical and writing tasks.
138	
139	**Parameters:**
140	| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
141	| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
142	| `prompt` | string | (required) | The task description and all necessary context. |
143	| `system_prompt` | string | (optional) | Persona or behavioral instruction for the model. |
144	| `temperature` | number | 0.7 | Controls randomness. Use 0.1-0.3 for factual tasks, 0.7-0.9 for creative tasks. |
145	| `max_tokens` | number | 8192 | Maximum output tokens. Increase to 16384 for longer outputs. |
146	
147	**When to Use:**
148	- Drafting documents, emails, or summaries exceeding 300 words.
149	- Synthesizing research from multiple sources into a report.
150	- Generating boilerplate content or templates.
151	- Performing initial analysis before deeper Pro-level investigation.
152	
153	**Good Example:** "Summarize these 10 meeting transcripts into a bulleted list of action items, grouped by responsible party."
154	**Bad Example:** "Write a complex recursive algorithm with memoization for graph traversal." (Use Pro instead.)
155	
156	**Edge Cases:**
157	- If Flash returns a shallow or incomplete analysis, retry with a more specific prompt before upgrading to Pro.
158	- For very long inputs (>100k tokens), ensure the prompt is well-structured with clear section markers.
159	
160	### ask_gemini_pro (Pro)
161	
162	**Role:** Deep reasoning, complex coding, and multi-step architectural planning.
163	
164	**Parameters:**
165	| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
166	| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
167	| `prompt` | string | (required) | Detailed technical requirements and context. |
168	| `system_prompt` | string | (optional) | Define the expert persona and constraints. |
169	| `temperature` | number | 0.7 | Use 0.1-0.3 for deterministic code, 0.5-0.7 for architectural design. |
170	| `max_tokens` | number | 16384 | Maximum output tokens. Sufficient for most code generation tasks. |
171	
172	**When to Use:**
173	- Code generation exceeding 50 lines.
174	- Complex algorithm implementation (recursion, graph algorithms, state machines).
175	- Multi-file refactoring with cross-reference awareness.
176	- Technical architecture documents with code examples.
177	
178	**Recommended System Prompts:**
179	- Code generation: "You are a senior software engineer. Write clean, well-documented code with proper error handling and type safety. Include JSDoc or docstring comments."
180	- Architecture: "You are a principal architect. Design systems for scalability, maintainability, and security. Provide concrete implementation details, not abstract descriptions."
181	- Refactoring: "You are a code reviewer. Identify anti-patterns, suggest improvements, and provide the refactored code. Preserve existing test interfaces."
182	
183	**Good Example:** "Refactor this 500-line monolithic function into a modular architecture using the Strategy pattern. Provide the full implementation with tests."
184	**Bad Example:** "What is the capital of France?" (Waste of Pro resources; use Flash or Claude directly.)
185	
186	**Error Handling:**
187	- If Pro returns a 429 (Rate Limit), implement a 30-second backoff, then fall back to Flash with a simplified version of the task.
188	- If Pro returns incomplete code (truncated output), increase `max_tokens` and retry.
189	
190	### web_search
191	
192	**Role:** Real-time information retrieval with Google Search grounding.
193	
194	**Parameters:**
195	| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
196	| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
197	| `search_query` | string | (required) | The search terms. Be specific and detailed. |
198	| `count` | integer | 10 | Number of results to return (1-50). Use 30-50 for deep research. |
199	| `search_recency_filter` | string | "noLimit" | Options: oneDay, oneWeek, oneMonth, oneYear, noLimit. |
200	| `search_domain_filter` | string | (optional) | Whitelist specific domains (comma-separated). |
201	
202	**When to Use:**
203	- Any research task requiring information beyond Claude's knowledge cutoff.
204	- Competitive intelligence gathering (use count=30-50).
205	- Verifying claims or checking current data (prices, statistics, events).
206	- Finding authoritative sources for a specific topic.
207	
208	**Good Example:** `web_search("enterprise AI adoption trends manufacturing 2025", count=30, search_recency_filter="oneMonth")`
209	**Bad Example:** `web_search("Python", count=5)` (Too generic; general knowledge does not require search.)
210	
211	**Edge Cases:**
212	- For niche topics, try multiple query variations if the first search yields poor results.
213	- Always pair with `web_reader` for full content extraction from top results.
214	
215	### web_reader
216	
217	**Role:** High-fidelity content extraction from specific URLs.
218	
219	**Parameters:**
220	| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
221	| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
222	| `url` | string | (required) | The target webpage URL. Must be valid http/https. |
223	| `return_format` | string | "markdown" | Options: markdown (best for LLM), text (plain text). |
224	| `with_images_summary` | boolean | false | Include summary of images found on the page. |
225	| `with_links_summary` | boolean | false | Include summary of links found on the page. |
226	
227	**Standard Workflow:**
228	1. `web_search` to discover relevant URLs.
229	2. `web_reader` on the top 3-5 URLs (call in parallel for speed).
230	3. `ask_gemini` to synthesize the extracted content into a structured analysis.
231	
232	**Edge Cases:**
233	- For paywalled sites, `web_reader` may return a login page or cookie consent. Claude must detect this and inform the user or try an alternative source.
234	- If a URL fails, try using `web_search` to find a cached version or alternative source.
235	- Some JavaScript-heavy single-page applications may not render content properly. Note this limitation to the user.
236	
237	### parse_document
238	
239	**Role:** Advanced OCR and structured data extraction from PDFs and images.
240	
241	**Parameters:**
242	| Parameter | Type | Default | Description |
243	| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
244	| `file_url` | string | (required) | URL of the file to parse (must be publicly accessible). |
245	| `return_format` | string | "markdown" | Options: markdown (default), text (plain text). |
246	| `parse_mode` | string | "auto" | Options: auto (automatic detection), ocr (force OCR), layout (preserve layout). |
247	
248	**When to Use:**
249	- Extracting text from scanned documents, PDFs with complex tables, or images of text.
250	- Processing diagrams, flowcharts, or screenshots of code.
251	- Analyzing multi-page reports or contracts for key clauses.
252	
253	**Good Example:** "Extract all data from the tables in this quarterly earnings PDF and return as structured markdown."
254	**Bad Example:** "Read this 1-page plain text file." (Standard file reading is faster and cheaper.)
255	
256	**Processing Pattern:**
257	1. Call `parse_document` to extract raw text/structure.
258	2. Pass the extracted content to `ask_gemini` for analysis, summarization, or comparison.
259	3. Claude writes the final output to disk.
